name: Beets
notes: Sow 1/2 inch deep. Thin to 3 inches apart once they reach 2 inches tall.

name: Carrots
notes: Requires loose soil. Keep surface moist constantly until germination (14-21 days).

name: Potatoes
notes: Plant seed pieces 4 inches deep. Hill soil around stems as they grow.

name: Sugar Snap Peas
notes: Provide a trellis. They prefer the cool April soil in Tipp City.

name: Broccoli
notes: Give them plenty of nitrogen. Watch for cabbage moths as it warms up.

name: Kale
notes: Very hardy. Can handle a light frost if one hits in late April.

name: Onions
notes: Plant sets 1 inch deep. Ensure good drainage to prevent bulb rot.

name: Tomatoes
notes: Wait for soil to be consistently 60°F. Provide heavy support.

name: Sweet Corn
notes: Plant in blocks of at least 4 rows for proper pollination.

name: Peppers
notes: They love the heat. Do not rush these into cold April soil.

name: Garlic
notes: Plant cloves 4 inches deep. Mulch heavily for winter.

name: Fall Kale
notes: Frost improves the flavor of kale.

name: Zucchini
notes: Fast grower. Watch for squash bugs and provide plenty of space for air circulation.

name: Cucumber
notes: Trellis for cleaner fruit and better space management on the farm.

name: Green Beans
notes: Succession plant every 2 weeks for a continuous harvest through summer.

name: Butternut Squash
notes: Great for storage. Ensure they have a long, warm season to cure properly.

name: Spinach
notes: Prefers the cold. Will bolt quickly once June heat hits Ohio.

name: Radishes
notes: The ultimate 'gap filler' crop. Can be interplanted with slower crops like carrots.

name: Swiss Chard
notes: More heat-tolerant than spinach or kale. Beautiful rainbow stems.

name: Fall Carrots
notes: Sow in late July/August. Mulch well to keep soil cool during germination.

name: Brussels Sprouts
notes: Long season crop. Flavor peaks after the first hard freeze in Tipp City.

name: Sugar Beets
notes: High calorie yield. Great for livestock fodder or processing if you're experimenting with self-sufficiency.

name: Watermelon
notes: Needs significant space and heat. Heavy mulch helps retain moisture in sandy-loam soils.

name: Okra
notes: Extremely heat tolerant. Harvest pods every 2 days once they start producing to keep the plant active.

name: Asparagus
notes: Perennial investment. Do not harvest the first year; let the ferns build root energy.

name: Cabbage
notes: Heavy feeder. Needs consistent watering to prevent heads from splitting.

name: Eggplant
notes: Wait for consistent night temps above 50°F. Watch for flea beetles early on.

name: Sweet Potatoes
notes: Plant in mounds. Unlike Irish potatoes, these love the peak Ohio summer heat.

name: Pumpkins
notes: Great for livestock supplement. Requires plenty of room for vines to sprawl.

name: Fall Broccoli
notes: Often more successful than spring broccoli in Ohio due to cooling autumn nights.

name: Hairy Vetch
notes: Cover crop. Nitrogen fixer that protects the soil over winter for the farm's regeneration.

name: Winter Rye
notes: Hardy cover crop. Prevents erosion and adds significant organic matter when tilled in spring.

name: Blueberries
notes: Requires acidic soil (pH 4.5-5.5). Ideal for permanent beds near the pond area.

name: Lavender
notes: Perennial. Needs excellent drainage. Attracts pollinators to the farm's vegetable blocks.

name: Crimson Clover
notes: Nitrogen-fixing cover crop. Beautiful red blooms in spring before you till it in.

name: Sunflowers
notes: Tall varieties can act as a natural windbreak or trellis for pole beans.

name: Strawberries
notes: June-bearing or everbearing. Great for regenerative ground cover in orchards.

name: Leeks
notes: Very cold hardy. Blanch the stems by hilling soil as they grow.

name: Turnips
notes: Dual-purpose: edible roots for the kitchen and greens for the helper's livestock.

name: Dill
notes: Self-seeds easily. Essential for pickling the cucumber harvest later.

name: Cantaloupe
notes: Requires high heat and consistent water. Watch for powdery mildew in late summer.

name: Rhubarb
notes: Perennial. Plant once and harvest for 10+ years. Extremely hardy in Ohio winters.

name: Pawpaw
notes: Ohio's state native fruit. Prefers low-light when young; needs a cross-pollinator nearby.

name: Elderberry
notes: Native perennial. Great for farm-made syrups. Grows well in the damper areas near your pond.

name: Cauliflower
notes: Trickier than broccoli. Needs blanching (tying leaves over the head) to keep the curd white.

name: Arugula
notes: Fast-growing 'peppery' green. Succession plant every 10 days for a consistent salad mix.

name: Cherry Tomatoes
notes: Easier and faster than slicers. High-yield for farm stands; very resistant to early blight.

name: Winter Squash (Delicata)
notes: Excellent storage life and gourmet flavor. Skin is edible, making it a customer favorite.

name: Raspberries
notes: Perennial bramble. Fall-bearing varieties provide fruit when other berries have finished.

name: Parsnips
notes: Slow to germinate. Leave in the ground through the first freeze to maximize sweetness.

name: Sorghum
notes: Highly drought-tolerant. Use for grain, syrup, or as a high-biomass regenerative cover crop.

name: Comfrey
notes: Dynamic accumulator. Deep roots bring up minerals; use leaves as mulch or livestock 'treats'.
